4.1 Nucleic Acids—Informational Macromolecules

Two Types of Nucleic Acid: D N A and R N A (1 of 5)
Chemical structure of ribonucleic acid (R N A) and deoxyribonucleic acid (D N A)
Figure 4.2 Chemical structures of ribonucleic acid (R N A) and deoxyribonucleic acid (D N A).

Two Types of Nucleic Acid: D N A and R N A (2 of 5)
Electron micrograph of D N A released from the bacteriophage T 2
Figure 4.1 A nucleic acid molecule visualized by electron microscopy.

Two Types of Nucleic Acid: D N A and R N A (3 of 5)
Purine and pyrimidine bases found in D N A and R N A
Figure 4.3 Purine and pyrimidine bases found in D N A and
R N A.

Two Types of Nucleic Acid: D N A and R N A (4 of 5)
Purine-containing nucleosides and nucleotides
Figure 4.4 Nucleosides and nucleotides.

Two Types of Nucleic Acid: D N A and R N A (5 of 5)
Pyrimidine-containing nucleosides and nucleotides
Figure 4.4 Nucleosides and nucleotides.

Properties of Nucleotides (1 of 4)
Ionization (or deprotonation) of nucleotides
Table 4.1 Ionization contants of ribonucleotides expressed as
values

Properties of Nucleotides (2 of 4)
Tautomerization of purine and pyrimidine bases
Figure 4.5 Tautomerization of the bases.

Properties of Nucleotides (3 of 4)
Nucleotides are strong acids
Primary ionization of phosphate at p H values close to 1.0
Secondary ionization of phosphate and protonation or deprotonation of amino group on the bases at neutral p H
Bases have tautomeric forms (amino vs. imino or keto vs. enol)
Light absorption in U V range (around 260 nm)

Properties of Nucleotides (4 of 4)
U V absorption spectra of ribonucleotides
Figure 4.6 Ultraviolet absorption spectra of ribonucleotides.
U V light absorption is used to detect and quantify nucleic acids

Phosphodiester Linkage (1 of 3)
Formation and stability
Polar molecules have no net charge but an asymmetric internal distribution of charge
They have a dipole moment
with fractional charges
In (aqueous) cellular environments, polar molecules can be attracted by nearby ions (charge-dipole interactions) or by other polar molecules (dipole-dipole interactions)

Phosphodiester Linkage (2 of 3)
Alkaline hydrolysis of R N A
Figure 4.7 Alkaline hydrolysis of R N A via a cyclic intermediate.
Hydrolytic steps proceed through a cyclic intermediate not possible in D N A

Phosphodiester Linkage (3 of 3)
Nucleic acid synthesis using activated nucleotides
Figure 4.8 Use of a metabolically activated nucleotide in nucleic acid biosynthesis.
4.2 Primary Structure of Nucleic Acids

Nature and Significance of Primary Structure
Primary: sequence of nucleotides
This strand has a
direction
Strand can also be drawn as p A p C p G p T p T - O H, p A p C p G p T p T (-O H is assumed) or A C G T T, conventional
representation is
end to the left

D N A as the Genetic Substance: Early Evidence (1 of 2)
In 1944, Avery and his colleagues showed that nonpathogenic pneumococci could be made pathogenic by the transfer of D N A from a pathogenic strain
Figure 4.9 Experiments that showed D N A to be the genetic substance.

D N A as the Genetic Substance: Early Evidence (2 of 2)
In 1952, Hershey and Chase showed that D N A transferred from a virus to a bacterium contains sufficient information to direct the synthesis of new viruses
Figure 4.9 Experiments that showed D N A to be the genetic substance.
4.3 Secondary and Tertiary Structures of Nucleic Acids

The D N A Double Helix (1 of 6)
Secondary: 3D arrangements of nucleotide residues with respect to one another, and short-term folding interactions such as the double helix
Tertiary: Longer range 3D interactions such as supercoiling, and superhelical forms such as cruciforms

The D N A Double Helix (2 of 6)
Figure 1.2 James Watson and Francis Crick with their hand-assembled wire model of the structure of D N A.
J. Watson and F. Crick in front of their D N A double helix model
Figure 4.10 Evidence for the structure of D N A.
X-ray fiber diffraction image of D N A generated by R. Franklin

The D N A Double Helix (3 of 6)
Base pairing
Figure 4.11 Fundamental elements of structure in the D N A double helix.
Chargaff’s rules; D N A base composition:

The D N A Double Helix (4 of 6)
Stacking of base pairs
Figure 4.11 Fundamental elements of structure in the D N A double helix.
angle between stacked
base pairs means 10 b p/turn

The D N A Double Helix (5 of 6)
Distance between base pairs
Figure 4.11 Fundamental elements of structure in the D N A double helix.
The rise of the helix
is the distance between successive base pairs (twice the van der Waals thickness of a planar ring)

The D N A Double Helix (6 of 6)
The space-filling model of D N A
This type of D N A model shows both close packing of atoms in the structure, and major and minor grooves
Figure 4.12 A space-filling model of D N A.

Semiconservative Nature of D N A Replication (1 of 3)
Figure 4.13 A model for D N A replication.
Each strand of the parent molecule acts as a template for a new, complementary strand

Semiconservative Nature of D N A Replication (2 of 3)
Three initial models of replication mechanism
Figure 4.14 Three models of D N A replication.

Semiconservative Nature of D N A Replication (3 of 3)
The Meselson–Stahl experiment (1958)
Figure 4.15 The Meselson–Stahl experiment proved that D N A replicates semiconservatively.
Growth in medium containing
as the sole N source
gives D N A of density 1.724 g/m l

The Meselson–Stahl Experiment (1958) (1 of 3)
Figure 4.15 The Meselson–Stahl experiment proved that D N A replicates semiconservatively.
Growth of E. coli in
standard medium gives D N A of
density 1.710 g/m l

The Meselson–Stahl Experiment (1958) (2 of 3)
Figure 4.15 The Meselson–Stahl experiment proved that D N A replicates semiconservatively.
After transfer of dense bacteria (1.724 g/m l) to light medium for one generation, all of the D N A had intermediate density (1.717 g/m l), as shown by C s C l density equilibrium centrifugation

The Meselson–Stahl Experiment (1958) (3 of 3)
Figure 4.15 The Meselson–Stahl experiment proved that D N A replicates semiconservatively.
Succeeding generations cultured in light medium show both the hybrid and light D N A

Alternative Nucleic Acid Structures: B and A Helices (1 of 2)
There are two major forms of D N A double helix: the B form (on the left) is the most common structural form of D N A in cells, while the A form (on the right) is seen in double-stranded R N A and in D N A-R N A hybrids
Figure 4.16 Comparison of the two major forms of D N A.

Alternative Nucleic Acid Structures: B and A Helices (2 of 2)
Crystal structure of B-D N A
The “Dickerson dodecamer” has ~10.4 base pairs per turn, some bases are tilted slightly, D N A helix is sometimes slightly bent
Figure 4.17 The structure of B- D N A from studies of molecular crystals.

D N A and R N A Molecules In Vivo (1 of 5)
Figure 4.18 A D N A molecule as seen by the electron microscope.
Electron microscopic presentation of a D N A molecule

D N A and R N A Molecules In Vivo (2 of 5)
Figure 4.18 A D N A molecule as seen by the electron microscope.
Relaxed and supercoiled D N A molecules
Supercoiled form on top and bottom, relaxed form in the middle

D N A and R N A Molecules In Vivo (3 of 5)
Figure 4.20 Creating supercoiled circular D N A.
Creating supercoiled circular D N A

D N A and R N A Molecules In Vivo (4 of 5)
Positive and negative supercoiling
L, linking number: number of times one strand crosses the other T, twist: number of turns W, writhe: number of
super helical turns
so long as no D N A strand is broken
Figure 4.21 Negative and positive supercoils.

D N A and R N A Molecules In Vivo (5 of 5)
Gel electrophoresis demonstrating D N A supercoiling
Topoisomerases are enzymes that modify state of D N A supercoiling. Their action can be followed by agarose gel electrophoresis in the presence of ethidium bromide (a fluorescent dye). The smaller or more compact a D N A molecule, the more rapid its migration
Figure 4.22 Gel electrophoresis demonstrating D N A supercoiling.

Single-Stranded Polynucleotides (1 of 3)
Common conformations
Most D N A molecules found in cells are double-stranded, but most naturally occurring R N A molecules are single-stranded
Figure 4.23 Conformations of single-stranded nucleic acids.

Single-Stranded Polynucleotides (2 of 3)
Structure of transfer R N A (t R N A)
Structure was determined by X-ray diffraction
Figure 4.25 The tertiary structure of a transfer R N A as determined by X-ray diffraction.

Single-Stranded Polynucleotides (3 of 3)
Self-complementarity and the structure of t R N A
Most R N A is single-stranded and could be a random coil except for areas of internal complementarity. Transfer R N A molecules used in protein synthesis (75-80 nucleotides long) have extensive regions of intramolecular complementarity, which fold in upon one another
Figure 4.24 How self-complementarity dictates the tertiary structure of a t R N A molecule.
4.4 Alternative Secondary Structures of D N A

Left-Handed D N A (Z-D N A)
Structure
In A- and B-D N A helices all nucleotides are in anti orientation, while in the left-handed Z-D N A helix, pyrimidines are anti and purines are syn
Figure 4.27 Syn and anti conformations for two nucleosides.

Palindromic D N A Sequences: Hairpins and Cruciforms
Figure 4.28 A palindromic D N A sequence.
Palindromic D N A sequences are symmetrical with respect to nucleotide sequence (same in both directions). The sequences to the right form a double hairpin, or cruciform conformation

Triple-Stranded D N A Structures
Figure 4.29 Triple-stranded D N A structures.

G-Quadruplexes
Figure 4.30 A G-quartet (a) and two views of a G-quadruplex (b) and (c).
G-quadruplexes probably exist in telomeres, the protective structures at the end of linear D N A molecules
4.5 The Helix-to-Random Coil Transition: Nucleic Acid Denaturation

Separation of Double-Stranded into Single-Stranded D N A (1 of 6)
is promoted by:
electrostatic repulsion (phosphate group negative charge) between chains (charge neutralized to some
extent by anions:
high ionic strength
stabilizes duplex structure
high entropic factor
in comparing flexible single-
strand chain with ordered duplex
alkali and heat

Separation of Double-Stranded into Single-Stranded D N A (2 of 6)
Enthalpic component
stabilizes duplex D N A:
H-bonding between complementary bases and van der Waals attraction between stacked bases
D N A denaturation is reversible: renaturation promoted by slow cooling; rapid cooling creates a population of single-stranded random coils
Helix-coil transition is cooperative: early structural changes promote later ones, and “melting” occurs over a small temperature range; an abrupt transition

Separation of Double-Stranded into Single-Stranded D N A (3 of 6)
Figure 4.31 Denaturation of D N A.
Heating double-stranded D N A denatures it into two higher energy single strands

Separation of Double-Stranded into Single-Stranded D N A (4 of 6)
Figure 4.31 Denaturation of D N A.
As T increases,
overcomes a positive
making
negative. Separation of double-stranded D N A becomes a favorable reaction

Separation of Double-Stranded into Single-Stranded D N A (5 of 6)
Figure 4.31 Denaturation of D N A.
Native D N A absorbs less light than denatured D N A

Separation of Double-Stranded into Single-Stranded D N A (6 of 6)
Figure 4.31 Denaturation of D N A.
This change in absorbance can be used to follow D N A denaturation, that is, separation of double-stranded D N A
4.6 The Biological Functions of Nucleic Acids: A Preview of Genetic Biochemistry

Storage of Genetic Information, Replication, Transcription, and Translation (1 of 5)
D N A is used to store the total genetic information of an organism (genome)
Replication passes on the genetic information from cell to cell and from generation to generation
Transcription converts D N A-based information of genes into messenger R N A (m R N A) molecules
Translation produces proteins based on information in m R N A at ribosomes

Storage of Genetic Information, Replication, Transcription, and Translation (2 of 5)
Replication and the D N A polymerase reaction
Figure 4.32 The D N A polymerase reaction.
D N A polymerase is one of a host of proteins in the replisome, a complex that replicates D N A

Storage of Genetic Information, Replication, Transcription, and Translation (3 of 5)
Figure 4.33 The basic principle of transcription.
Conversion of D N A into R N A
Transcription (the production of an R N A transcript) is the first process in the readout of information encoded in D N A

Storage of Genetic Information, Replication, Transcription, and Translation (4 of 5)
Figure 4.34 The basic principle of translation.
Protein synthesis
In translation, the sequence of nucleotides in an m R N A molecule provides the information for the synthesis of a protein at the ribosome

Storage of Genetic Information, Replication, Transcription, and Translation (5 of 5)
Flow of genetic information in a typical cell
Figure 4.35 The flow of genetic information in a typical cell.
4.7 Tools of Biochemistry

Manipulating D N A (1 of 2)
Common biochemical applications to manipulate genetic information encoded within D N A are:
gene cloning
chemical synthesis of oligonucleotides of defined sequence
D N A sequence analysis
site-directed mutagenesis
polymerase chain reaction (P C R)

Manipulating D N A (2 of 2)
Figure 4A.1 Creation of a recombinant D N A molecule in vitro.

Cloning a D N A Fragment into a Plasmid Vector
Figure 4A.3 p B R 322, one of the earliest cloning vectors.
Figure 4A.2 Cloning a fragment of D N A into a plasmid vector and introducing the recombinant molecule into bacteria.

Chemical Synthesis of Oligonucleotides (1 of 2)
Figure 4A.4 The coupling and oxidation steps in the solid-phase synthesis of oligonucleotides by the phosphoramidite method.
Coupling and oxidation steps in the solid-phase synthesis of oligonucleotides with defined sequences by the phosphoramidite method

Chemical Synthesis of Oligonucleotides (2 of 2)
Design and synthesis of 3D nanostructures
Figure 4A.5 Design and synthesis of three-dimensional nanostructures.

Dideoxynucleotide Sequence Analysis (1 of 2)
Figure 4A.6 D N A sequencing by the Sanger method.

Dideoxynucleotide Sequence Analysis (2 of 2)
Automated D N A sequencing
Figure 4A.7 Data from a D N A sequencing gel.
All four base-specific termination reactions are analyzed in one gel by use of d d N T Ps modified with fluorescent dyes; each d d N T P receives a unique color

Site-Directed Mutagenesis
Site-directed mutagenesis using M13 phage as a cloning vector. M13 is a single-strand D N A phage that replicates via a double-stranded D N A replicative intermediate
Figure 4A.8 Use of a mismatched synthetic oligonucleotide primer to introduce mutations in a gene cloned into a single-stranded vector.

X-Ray Diffraction
Figure 4B.1 Diffraction from a very simple structure—a row of atoms or molecules.

Fiber Diffraction (1 of 2)
Resolving the structure of a simple helical molecule
Characteristics of a helical molecule:
Repeat – distance between repeating units
Pitch – distance measured for one helical turn
Rise – distance from one residue to another
Figure 4B.2 A simple helical molecule.

Fiber Diffraction (2 of 2)
Using a helical polymer model
Figure 4B.3 Diffraction from fibers.

Crystal Diffraction (1 of 2)
Figure 4B.5 Diffraction pattern produced by a crystal of a small D N A molecule.
Molecules in a unit cell
Diffraction pattern of a small D N A crystal
Figure 4B.6 Part of an electron density map derived from the crystal diffraction pattern shown in Figure 4B.5.
Partial electron density map derived from the diffraction pattern

Crystal Diffraction (2 of 2)
A model structure (G r o E L) at different resolutions
Figure 4B.7 Models of the structure of G r o E L (a chaperone protein; see Chapter 6) at resolutions (left to right) of 4, 8, and

Chapter 4 Summary (1 of 3)
There are two types of biologically relevant nucleic acid: D N A and R N A
R N A has the sugar ribose, while D N A has deoxyribose
R N A uses the pyrimidine uracil, while D N A uses thymine
Both, R N A and D N A, are polymers of four kinds of nucleoside 5′-phosphates connected by links between
3′-hydroxyls and 5′-phosphates
Each nucleic acid has a defined sequence, the primary structure

Chapter 4 Summary (2 of 3)
D N A is the polymer of genetic information
D N A has a secondary structure of a right-handed double helix
The purine adenine basepairs via two hydrogen bonds with the pyrimidine thymine, while the purine guanine basepairs via three hydrogen bonds with the pyrimidine cytosine
The replication mechanism is semiconservative
Energy needs to be invested to separate the double helical D N A structure into single strands

Chapter 4 Summary (3 of 3)
Both, D N A and R N A, form various tertiary structures such as supercoils, hairpins, or cruciform
Nucleic acids have several important biological functions:
D N A contains stored genetic information
D N A is transcribed into messenger R N A (m R N A)
m R N A is translated into proteins at the ribosome
Several tools and techniques have been developed to manipulate D N A such as gene cloning, automated oligonucleotide synthesis, dideoxynucleotide sequencing, site-directed mutagenesis, and X-ray diffraction
